segments.s328-g41: به دست چپش is a locative phrase: به + noun + possessive enclitic (چپ‑+‑ش = 'his left side'), literally 'at/on his left hand/side'. رستم پهلوان follows in apposition (name + epithet); word order (prepositional phrase before the noun phrase) is typical of narrative listing—no copula is expressed (‘stood’ is implied).
segments.s328-g42: ز کابل is a poetic prepositional phrase meaning 'from Kabul' (ز = 'from'). بزرگان روشن‌روان is a nominal phrase ('the great ones, bright‑souled'), with روشن‌روان an adjectival epithet applied to بزرگان; the whole segment names those who come from Kabul.
segments.s328-g43: به پیش اندرون combines به (locative) + پیش اندرون ('before/in front of the inner quarters' or 'in the presence of the inner court'); the following proper names (طوس و گودرز و گیو) are the people located there. The prepositional phrase sets the scene; the names are a coordinated list in apposition to that location.
segments.s328-g44: چو here means 'like' or 'as' and introduces a further list of names (رهام و شاپور و گرگین). The final word نیو appears to be a name in the text; the coordinating و joins the items. چو is a classical/poetic connective used to compare or continue enumeration.
segments.s328-g45: پس functions as a clause‑linking adverb 'then/after/next'; پشت او = 'behind him' (پشت + pronoun او). The names بیژن و گستهم form a coordinated subject or list following that locative. Note: گستهم here is a proper name (Gostaham) despite the provided sense list not showing that meaning.
segments.s328-g46: بزرگان که بودند با او به هم is a relative clause: بزرگان ('the great ones') + که (relative 'who') + بودند (past plural 'they were') + با او ('with him') + به هم ('together'/'in company'). The clause states that these great men were together with him; بود- is the past copula/auxiliary in plural form.
segments.s328-g25: This is a short clause with an omitted subject (they). سراپرده is the patient/object 'the inner pavilion/curtain-chamber' and از شهر بیرون is a prepositional phrase 'out of the city' placed before the verb. برید is an archaic/poetic past form of بردن ('to take/carry')—here third-person plural—so the clause means 'they brought/drew the pavilion out of the city.'
segments.s328-g26: درفش همایون is the direct object 'the auspicious/royal standard' (درفش modified by همایون). به هامون is a directional prepositional phrase 'into/towards the plain.' The verb برید again is the poetic past of بردن: 'they carried/brought.' Word order places object + direction before the verb, typical of Persian poetic style.
segments.s328-g27: ز here functions as the poetic preposition 'from.' The phrase lists origins (ز خرگاه وز خیمه) 'from the hall/palace and from the tents'; وز is an archaic/linking form here (best read as ‘and/from’) connecting the two nouns. چندان که هست is a fixed expression 'as many/as much as there is,' with که introducing the clause and هست as the copula/existential.
segments.s328-g28: بسازید is a second-person plural imperative/jussive 'prepare/make' (addressing those who arrange the camp). بر دشت is the locative 'on the plain,' followed by جای نشست 'places for sitting/settling' (جای + infinitive/nominalized action). The verb is plural and governs the whole phrase: 'prepare places to sit on the plain.'
segments.s328-g29: This is a noun list serving as the object of an implied verb: درفش بزرگان و پیل و سپاه = 'the banners/standards of the nobles, and the elephants, and the army.' The coordinating و simply links the items in the list. The phrase enumerates what is to be arranged or displayed.
segments.s328-g30: بسازید again is the imperative/plural 'set up/prepare.' روشن functions as an adjective 'clear/bright' modifying the following noun phrase; یکی is the numeral 'one/a single' and directly qualifies رزمگاه 'battlefield/place of battle.' The order (adjective + numeral + noun) is a poetic arrangement meaning 'make one clear battlefield.'
segments.s328-g31: چنان کرد رستم is a simple past clause 'Rostam did thus/acted so' (چنان + کرد). The conjunction که introduces reported content or manner: 'چنان کرد رستم که خسرو بگفت' = 'Rostam did so, as Khosrow had said.' بگفت is a past-tense reporting verb 'said/told,' so the whole clause links Rostam's action to the king's instruction.
segments.s328-g32: ببردند is the full third-person plural past form 'they took/brought' (here with the -ند ending). پرده‌سرای is the object 'curtained-hall/chamber' (a curtained pavilion/room), and از نهفت is a prepositional phrase 'out from concealment' (نهفت = hidden/secret). Together: 'they brought the curtained chamber out of hiding.'
segments.s328-g33: Word order is subject after the locative phrase: به هامون (to the hamun) + کشیدند (they moved/drew) with ایرانیان (the subject) placed after the verb for poetic inversion. کشیدند is past‑tense, 3rd person plural (they), here meaning 'advanced/moved toward' rather than literal 'pulled'.
segments.s328-g34: ببستند is the past, 3rd person plural of بستن ('they shut/closed'); the prefixed ب- is a classical/perfective form but functions as simple past here. به فرمان is an ablative/instrumental phrase 'by command' (به = 'by' here), and یکسر modifies the verb meaning 'entirely/altogether'; میان is the direct object 'the middle/center' (i.e. they sealed off the central passage).
segments.s328-g35: A simple enumerative list of colors joined by و (and) for poetic effect. Each adjective is used as a color descriptor; word order is typical (adjectives listed in sequence).
segments.s328-g36: زمین (the land/ground) is the topic, followed by a frame 'کوه تا کوه' ('from mountain to mountain' — تا here = 'as far as'/'from...to'), then the predicate پر خیمه بود ('was full of tents'): پر + noun = 'full of [noun]', and بود is past ('was').
segments.s328-g37: میان اندرون is a locative phrase 'in the midst/within the inner (quarters)'; کاویانی qualifies درفش ('the Kayanian/Kayanian‑dynasty banner'). Word order places the descriptive adjective (کاویانی) before the noun درفش, as is normal for attributive adjectives in classical Persian.
segments.s328-g38: This clause uses ز + او (written here as ز ... شده) to mean 'from it / because of it' (زو = 'from it'), followed by شده ('has become' / 'was made') and a color list as complements. جهان is the affected domain 'the world/scene'; شده is a past participle indicating change of state ('became').
segments.s328-g39: A noun phrase: سراپرده زال ('Zal's inner tent/inner chamber') followed by the locative نزدیک شاه ('near the king'). The possessive relation is implicit: 'the inner tent of Zal'. No finite verb appears in this segment (poetic nominal phrase).
segments.s328-g40: برافراخته is a past/passive participial adjective meaning 'raised'/'erected'; it functions predicatively here. The sequence ز + و (written separately in the word list) corresponds to the poetic clitic 'زو' = 'from it', i.e. 'from that tent'; درفش سیاه is 'the black banner' (darfash + adj.). Overall: 'From it the black banner was raised.'
segments.s328-g17: بیازید is a past/poetic verb meaning 'broke forth/let out' (an intransitive sudden action); و simply connects the clauses ('and'). بگرفت is a past tense (he took/ seized) formed with the prefix ب- plus گرفت; دستش به دست is a doubled construction meaning 'his hand into (another) hand' — literally 'took his hand into (his) hand,' i.e. 'took him by the hand.' Word order is Verb + object (pronoun attached to دست as -ش).
segments.s328-g18: بر خویش بردش is a sequence where بر (toward/to) + خویش ('his own' / reflexive 'to himself') combines with بردش (he led/took him — here an ordinary past of بردن, though the provided lemma senses do not list the 'take' sense). به جای نشست is 'he/they set (him) down in a place' where به جای = 'into/at the place' and نشست is the past of نشستن used transitively here as 'to seat/settle.' Overall the clause means 'he drew him to his side and sat him down in his place.'
segments.s328-g19: بدانست is a past tense compound meaning 'he knew/realized.' کاو here is an archaic connective/relative meaning 'that' or 'who' introducing the subordinate clause. این سخن = 'this speech' and جز به مهر = 'except by/for love' (i.e. 'only out of affection'). The clause reads 'he knew that this speech was prompted only by love.'
segments.s328-g20: نپیمود is a negative past form (نـ + پیمود) meaning 'did not [do/undertake]' or 'was not in accord' — in context it qualifies the previous clause: 'he would not (have acted) except by affection.' با introduces the beneficiary/partner 'with the king,' and خورشید چهر is a compound epithet 'sun-faced' (خورشید = sun, چهر = face/appearance) describing the king. Word order is Verb (negative past) + prepositional phrase.
segments.s328-g21: چنین گفت is a common formula 'thus said' (چنین = 'thus/so', گفت here functions as past 'said' even though the supplied senses list is nominal — it is the verb گفتن in past). پس adds a temporal/sequential 'then.' شاه با زال زر = 'the king with Zal‑Zar' or 'the king said to Zal, the golden‑haired' (با introduces the addressee), where زر is an epithet 'gold' qualifying Zal.
segments.s328-g22: که here introduces direct speech/command ('that'/'saying that'): اکنون = 'now'. ببندید is a 2nd‑person plural/polite imperative 'bind/gird' (بـ + بَندید), یکسر is an adverb 'at once/entirely', and کمر means 'waist/girdle' (here 'gird your loins'). The structure is the king's command: 'Now gird your loins at once.'
segments.s328-g23: This is a simple nominative list of persons joined by و (and): تو (you), رستم (Rostam), طوس (Tūs—here the hero, not the city), گودرز (Goudarz) and گیو (Giv). The repeated و simply coordinates the names; the sequence functions as the addressees/participants.
segments.s328-g24: دگر هر که = 'and/other — whoever' (دگر 'other/another', هر که 'whoever'); او نامدارست = 'he is famed' (او = he, نامدارست = is renowned). نیو at the end appears as a name/term appended to the list (its exact nuance depends on manuscript reading). The whole phrase enumerates 'and any other who is renowned (Niyu).' Word order is pronoun/relative + predicate, closing with the name.
segments.s328-g1: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a subordinate clause. دستان is the personal name (here not matched to the listed noun senses). شنید is simple past 'heard' (SOV order: subject دستان + verb شنید with object following). این سخن is the object 'these/this words', and خیره شد is a two-part predicate 'became fixed/stared/was stunned' where خیره is a participial adjective and شد is the past copula 'became'.
segments.s328-g2: همی is an emphatic/progressive particle here (classical usage giving immediacy). چشمش is 'his eye/gaze' with the possessive suffix; از روی او is a prepositional phrase 'from/at his face/toward him'; تیره شد is 'became dark' (تیره = dark + شد = past 'became').
segments.s328-g3: خروشان شد is a poetic periphrasis 'became roaring' = 'a clamor/uproar arose'; از شاه marks the source 'from the king'. و بر پای خاست is 'and (he) rose to his feet' with the older past form خاست ('arose') following the locative phrase بر پای.
segments.s328-g4: چنین گفت is the common formula 'thus said' (چنین 'thus'). کای is a vocative 'O' used to address someone; here it introduces داور 'judge'. داد و راست are nouns 'justice and truth/right' placed after the vocative as qualities invoked. Note: گفت here functions as 'said' though the provided sense list did not include the verbal sense.
segments.s328-g5: ز من بود is a verbless/catenative construction 'was from me' (بو د is past copula). تیزی و نابخردی are two coordinated nouns 'sharpness/keenness and foolishness', the things the speaker admits originate with himself.
segments.s328-g6: توی here functions as an archaic/vocative marker equivalent to 'تو ای' — addressing the addressee ('O you'). پاک فرزانه ایزدی is a string of epithets 'pure, wise, divine' modifying that addressee; word order is epithet sequence followed by the noun implied (one).
segments.s328-g7: سزدگر is a conditional/optative formula 'if it be fitting' (lit. 'fitting‑if'); ببخشی is the 2nd-person present/subjunctive 'you forgive' used in a polite/requesting sense. گناه مرا is the object 'my sin' (here written with clitic split into م + را in the tokenization).
segments.s328-g8: اگر introduces a subordinate conditional 'if'. دیو is the subject 'the demon'; گم کرد is a causative phrasing 'caused (to be) lost' or 'made (me) lose', where کرد functions as a causative verb and گم is the state 'lost/astray'. راه مرا is the direct object 'my way/path' with the clitic m (me/my) separated in the tokenization.
segments.s328-g9: مرا سالیان شد فزون از شمار is a temporal/experiential construction: مرا (to me / for me) marks the experiencer, سالیان شد is an existential/past construction 'years have been' (شد used as an auxiliary/existential), and فزون از شمار follows as a postmodifier meaning 'more than can be counted' (literally 'greater than number'). The phrase as a whole = 'for years more than number (to me)' → 'for many years, beyond counting.'
segments.s328-g10: کمر بسته‌ام پیش هر شهریار: بسته‌ام is a perfect/past participle with the 1st-person clitic '-ام' meaning 'I have bound' (here idiomatically 'I have girded my loins'); کمر names the part girded ('waist' / 'girdle') and precedes the verb as object; پیش هر شهریار is a prepositional phrase 'in the presence of / before every king' (پیش as 'in the presence of').
segments.s328-g11: ز شاهان ندیدم کز این‌گونه راه is built with ز شاهان 'from/among the kings' as the domain, ندیدم 'I did not see' (past tense, subject implied), and کز introducing a relative/subordinate clause ('that/such that'). این‌گونه 'this kind of / such' modifies راه 'path/course' which is the head of the relative clause; poetic order places the negated main verb before the subordinate clause.
segments.s328-g12: بجستی is past (perfect) 2nd/3rd-person form of بجستن (here 'to take/choose/seek a path') — 'you/one took up'; ز دادار خورشید و ماه is a noun phrase 'from/of the Creator, (the) Sun and Moon' (ز marking origin/agent or source), where خورشید و ماه are coordinated nouns modifying or governed by دادار. The clause completes the relative begun by کز in the previous segment.
segments.s328-g13: که introduces this clause ('that' / 'for') and ما را is a dative/experiencer marking ('to us' / 'for us' — را here marks the affected party rather than a simple direct object). جدایی 'separation' is the noun-predicate and نبود is the negative past existential 'there was not' (here 'we had no ...'). آرزوی ('wish') completes the predicate: literally 'there was no wish (for) separation to us.' Word order is inverted for poetic effect.
segments.s328-g14: از این دادگر خسرو نیک‌خوی is a prepositional/attributive phrase modifying the desire: از 'from/of' introduces the source (here 'from this just ...'), این 'this' points to the previously mentioned ruler, دادگر is an epithet 'just ruler', خسرو is the proper name, and نیک‌خوی is an adjective 'kind‑natured / good‑dispositioned' placed after the name (poetic/nominal modifier order).
segments.s328-g15: سخنهای دستان چو بشنید شاه: سخنهای 'the words/talk' takes دستان as the genitive/possessive ('of Dastan' or 'of the tales' depending on sense), چو is temporal 'when', and بشنید is past 'he heard'. Note the verb precedes the subject شاه (typical poetic inversion): 'when he heard the words of Dastan, (then) the king ...'.
segments.s328-g16: پسند آمدش is an impersonal/idiomatic structure: پسند 'approval/liking' + آمدش 'came to him' = 'it pleased him'. پوزش is the object 'pardon/excuse', and نیک‌خواه postmodifies it as 'of the well-wisher / benevolent one'. The word-order (noun + آمدش) is idiomatic: 'the pardon of the well-wisher came to him (i.e. pleased him)'.

sentences.s328-s21: On his left hand stood Rostam the champion, the great, bright‑souled men from Kabul.
sentences.s328-s22: Before the inner (chamber) were Tous, Goudarz and Giv, and like them Roham, Shapur and Gorgin — (and) Niu.
sentences.s328-s23: Behind him were Bijan and Gostaham, the great ones who were together with him.
sentences.s328-s13: They drew the inner pavilion out of the city and carried the auspicious standard into the plain.
sentences.s328-s14: From the palace and from the tents, as many as there are, prepare on the plain places to sit.
sentences.s328-s15: Set up the nobles' standards, the elephants and the host; prepare a single clear battlefield.
sentences.s328-s16: Rostam acted thus, as Khosrow had said; they brought the curtained chamber out from concealment.
sentences.s328-s17: The Iranians moved onto the hamun (plain); by command they completely closed off the middle.
sentences.s328-s18: White and black and purple and blue — the land, from mountain to mountain, was full of tents.
sentences.s328-s19: Within the inner quarters stood the Kayanian banner; because of it the world had become red and yellow and purple.
sentences.s328-s20: Zal's inner tent, near the king; from it the black banner was raised.
sentences.s328-s9: He broke forth and took him by the hand, drew him to his side, and sat him down in his place.
sentences.s328-s10: He perceived that this speech was prompted by nothing but affection for the sun‑faced king.
sentences.s328-s11: Thus the king said to Zal‑Zar: 'Now gird your loins at once.'
sentences.s328-s12: You and Rostam and Tous and Goudarz and Giv — and any other who is famed, Niyu.
sentences.s328-s1: When Dastan heard these words he stood stunned; his eyes darkened toward him.
sentences.s328-s2: A tumult rose from the king and he sprang to his feet; thus he said, 'O judge, justice and truth...'
sentences.s328-s3: Sharpness and folly were from me, O pure, wise, divine one.
sentences.s328-s4: If it is fitting, forgive my sin, if a demon has caused my way to go astray.
sentences.s328-s5: For years beyond counting I have girded my loins before every king.
sentences.s328-s6: Of the kings I have seen none who has taken such a course from the Creator, the Sun and the Moon.
sentences.s328-s7: for we had no desire to be parted from this just, kind-hearted King Khosrow.
sentences.s328-s8: When the king heard Dastan's words, the well-wisher's pardon pleased him.
